title: How the Rule-Breaking Octopus Is Rewriting the Evolution of Intelligence
gist: An iScience study of 79 cephalopod species proposes an 'asocial brain hypothesis': octopuses evolved large brains despite solitary, even cannibalistic lives, driven by habitat complexity rather than social group size, directly challenging the dominant social-brain (Machiavellian intelligence) theory.

insights.claim: The social brain (Machiavellian intelligence) hypothesis holds that big brains evolved to manage large social groups, tracking friends, foes, hierarchies, and shifting alliances, and in many mammals social group size correlates with brain size.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: States the reigning theory the study aims to overturn.
insights.locator: One theory, the social brain hypothesis

insights.claim: Cephalopods break the social-brain rule: octopuses have huge brains yet live solitary lives, mate without pair bonds, do not parent young, and can be territorial and cannibalistic, while still using tools, solving problems, and playing.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A clean counterexample undermining the universality of the social-brain theory.
insights.locator: But there's at least one big exception

insights.claim: An iScience study proposes an 'asocial brain hypothesis,' a variant of the cultural brain hypothesis, holding that big brains evolved to handle information learned asocially, not just socially.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Introduces an alternative mechanism for the evolution of intelligence.
insights.locator: an international team of researchers proposed

insights.claim: Analyzing 79 cephalopod species, researchers found ecological factors, especially habitat complexity, correlated with big brains, while sociality showed no correlation.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Empirical support locating brain evolution in ecology, not sociality.
insights.locator: the team compiled data on cephalopod brain size

insights.claim: Cephalopods living on the ocean floor and in shallow pools, where cleverness is rewarded with calorie-rich prey, tended to have larger brains.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Ties brain size to foraging payoff in complex habitats.
insights.locator: Cephalopods that lived on the ocean floor

insights.claim: Study author Michael Muthukrishna of the London School of Economics says cephalopods reveal there is another path to bigger brains beyond the dominant social story.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Names the researcher and the paradigm-shift claim.
insights.quote: Cephalopods reveal that there's another path to bigger brains.
